title: How Enid Survived an EF-4
hook: An EF-4 tornado tore through Enid, Oklahoma, and the shocking part is that no one died.

scenes.text: An EF-4 tornado tore through Enid, Oklahoma, and the shocking part is that no one died.
scenes.visualDirection: Open with a hard, urgent storm card that frames the survival story before the stats.

scenes.text: This was not a near miss. The storm packed one hundred seventy to one hundred seventy five mile per hour winds and carved a violent path straight through town.
scenes.visualDirection: Escalate with the storm intensity, emphasizing the violence and the scale.

scenes.text: It stayed on the ground for about nine miles, stretched roughly five hundred yards wide, and damaged around forty homes in and around Enid.
scenes.visualDirection: Use clean data-forward framing with path length, width, and home damage called out visually.

scenes.text: Emergency crews searched homes, pulled trapped residents out, and officials said the city somehow escaped with only minor injuries instead of mass casualties.
scenes.visualDirection: Shift into emergency response and rescue energy instead of spectacle.

scenes.text: That is why this story matters. Usually when damage looks this severe, there are deaths. Enid got extremely lucky.
scenes.visualDirection: Slow down and underline the contrast between the visible destruction and the outcome.

scenes.text: The lesson is not that the storm was weaker than it looked. The lesson is that preparation, shelter, and response made the difference when the worst case almost happened.
scenes.visualDirection: End on a clear takeaway card about preparation, shelter, and response.

reasoning: Manual latest-news script built from April 24, 2026 reporting on the Enid tornado so the isolated skill-pack project can render a real short without waiting on rate-limited LLM stages.
cta: Preparation is boring right up until it saves a town.
